docs.title: How to get started
docs.text:
First install the following nugget package:

dotnet new install Ardalis.CleanArchitecture.Template

Then create just create a new project using this template:

dotnet new clean-arch -o Downops.CheckInOps

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
vault operator inint

then save the token and the unseal keys. after that run:

vault operator unseal [KEY 1] \
vault operator unseal [KEY 2] \
vault operator unseal [KEY 3] \
vault login [TOKEN]

enable secrets:

vault secrets enable -path=secret kv-v2

put secrets in vault:

vault kv put secret/wallet-tracker/config MARIADB_ROOT_PASSWORD="your_password"  DATABASE_NAME="wallet_tracker"

docs.title: Create Cloudflare tunnel via CLI
docs.text:
First login:

sudo cloudflared login

then create the tunnel:

sudo cloudflared tunnel create my-tunnel

configure the file to indicate the service you are exposing. /root/.cloudflared/config.yml:

tunnel: TUNNEL_NAME
credentials-file: /root/.cloudflared/<tunnel-id>.json

ingress:
  - hostname: jenkins.example.com
    service: http://localhost:8080
  - service: http_status:404

then i have to configure the tunnel so it redirects to my service with the following http put request:

sudo cloudflared tunnel route dns my-tunnel test.downops.win

notice maybe you have to delete old dns records for your domain before the step above. then you just run the tunnel:

sudo cloudflared tunnel run my-tunnel

docs.title: Guide to create cloudflared tunnel
docs.text: After creating this tunnel a few times, I think that the easiest and most straight forward is to just go to the cloudflare dashboard, create the tunnel, then create the service in the linux server, and configure the dns route, thats it. You can see this in the following guide

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: apk add certbot certbot-dns-cloudflare nginx

docs.title: Nginx
docs.text:
At least a http virtualhost is required so the certbot can do the challenge to issue the certificate: /etc/nginx/http.d/wallettracker.conf

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name api.wallettracker.downops.win;
location /.well-kown/acme-challenge/ {
        root /var/www/certbot;
    }
    location / {
        return 301 https://$host$request_uri;
    }
}

docs.title: Certificate
docs.text:
Create the file and folder mkdir -p /root/.secrets/certbot vi /root/.secrets/certbot/cloudflare.ini

dns_cloudflare_api_token = API_TOKEN

Run

certbot certonly --dns-cloudflare --dns-cloudflare-credentials /root/.secrets/certbot/cloudflare.ini -d api.wallettracker.downops.win --non-interactive --agree-tos -m nnag@downops.win

docs.title: Nginx
docs.text:
Back to Nginx. After successfully creating the certificate, we can configure the virtualhost to use it, and also remove the certbot challenge from the http block:

server {                                             
    listen 443 ssl;                                  
    server_name api.wallettracker.downops.win;

    ssl_certificate /etc/letsencrypt/live/api.wallett
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/api.wal

    ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3;                   
    ssl_ciphers HIGH:!aNULL:!MD5;                    

    location / {                                     
        include uwsgi_params;                        
        uwsgi_pass unix:/tmp/app.sock;               
    }                                                
}                                                    

server {                                      
    listen 80;                                       
    return 301 https://$host$request_uri;            
}

nginx s -reload

docs.title: Cloudflared
docs.text:
Configure the tunnel: root/cloudflared/config.yml

tunnel: TUNNEL_ID         
credentials-file: /root/.cloudflared/TUNNEL_ID.json

ingress:                                   
  - hostname: api.wallettracker.downops.win
    service: https://127.0.0.1:443
    originRequest:          
      originServerName: api.wallettracker.downops.win
  - service: http_status:404

Run the tunnel as a background service:

rc-update add cloudflared default
rc-service cloudflared start

docs.title: Download binary
docs.text: wget https://github.com/cloudflare/cloudflared/releases/latest/download/cloudflared-linux-amd64 -O /usr/bin/cloudflared && chmod +x /usr/bin/cloudflared

docs.title: Create service
docs.text:
#!/sbin/openrc-run
name="cloudflared"
description="Cloudflare Tunnel"
command="/usr/bin/cloudflared"
command_args="tunnel run --token TUNNEL_TOKEN"
command_background=true
pidfile="/run/cloudflared.pid"
output_log="/var/logs/cloudflared.log"
error_log="/var/logs/cloudflared.log"

depend() {
  need net
}

docs.title: Start service
docs.text:
chmod +x /etc/init.d/cloudflared
rc-update add cloudflared default
rc-service cloudflared start

docs.title: Utils
docs.text:
• docker compose down -v shuts down containers, networks, and with -v volumes.
  • docker compose up -d --build builds and starts containers, networks, and volumes

docs.title: Docker-compose.yml
docs.text:
This is how i create two containers that comunicate with each other with a docker-compose.yml

version: "3.9"
services:
    db:
        build:
        context: ./mysql
        dockerfile: Dockerfile
        env_file:
        - .env
        ports:
        - "3306:3306"
        volumes:
        - db_data:/var/lib/mysql
        command: >
        sh -c "envsubst < /docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql.template > /docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql &&
exec docker-entrypoint.sh mysqld"
    app:
        build:
        context: ./app
        dockerfile: Dockerfile
        env_file:
        - .env
        ports:
        - "5000:5000"
        depends_on:
        - db
volumes:
db_data:

As I can read in this file generated by chatgpt, I notice there is a version definition with 3.9 since its the latest according to this website. Also if the version you include is obsolete you get a warning, so its ok to use this one.

Then i notice the services property, where you basically define the different Dockerfiles you'll run. Also, the name you put inside the service attribute is the hostname, that's an important consideration.

It's important to not that in the dbservice, the volume attribute is important, since thanks to it, our data is saved even though we restart the containers with docker compose down (which removes the containers, and networks). Also I want to point that the db_data:/var/lib/mysql is just a volume, a shared folder named db_data that points to /var/lib/mysql, key point here, is to notice that this docker container specifically stores its data here, that leads me to think that its also important to understand the container you are working with, not only the docker CLI and its file's syntax.

Also, in this context, inside db (mysql container) attribute, we have a lot of attributes, I'll just focus on the command attribute since I think is the least intuitive attribute:

sh -c "envsubst < /docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql.template > /docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql &&
exec docker-entrypoint.sh mysqld"

This command basically sends the content of init.sql.templateto envsubst, this previous mentioned instruction basically replaces the environment variables placeholders of the sql template with the real values of the current env. Then sends it to a file inside the container than later on, gets executed. This script basically creates the initial database so the application actually works.

docs.title: Flask app
docs.text:
FROM python:3.12-alpine
RUN apk update && \
apk add --no-cache mariadb-dev gcc musl-dev build-base linux-headers
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
RUN apk del gcc musl-dev build-base linux-headers
COPY . .
EXPOSE 5000
CMD ["uwsgi", "--ini", "uwsgi.ini"]
  • WORKDIR the working directory
  • EXPOSE port exposed to the host
  • RUN This commands are executed during the build process
  • CMD This commands run when the container is already executed

docs.title: Create certificate
docs.text:
apk add nginx openssl \
mkdir -p /etc/nginx/ssl \
cat > openssl.cnf <<EOF [req] distinguished_name = req_distinguished_name x509_extensions = v3_req prompt = no [req_distinguished_name] CN = 100.96.42.211 [v3_req] subjectAltName = IP:100.96.42.211 EOF \
openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 365 -newkey rsa:2048 \
-keyout /etc/nginx/ssl/registry.key \
-out /etc/nginx/ssl/registry.crt \
-config openssl.cnf -extensions v3_req

docs.title: Configure nginx
docs.text:
add this config to /etc/nginx/http.d/registry.conf:

server {
    listen 443 ssl;
    server_name 100.96.42.211;

    ssl_certificate /etc/nginx/ssl/registry.crt;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/nginx/ssl/registry.key;

    # Ajuste para permitir subida de imágenes grandes
    client_max_body_size 0;

    # Chunked transfer encoding para Docker
    chunked_transfer_encoding on;

    location /v2/ {
        # No permitir versiones viejas de Docker
        if ($http_user_agent ~* (Docker/1.3|Docker/1.4|Docker/1.5|Docker/1.6)) {
            return 404;
        }

        proxy_pass                          http://localhost:5000;
        proxy_set_header  Host              $http_host;
        proxy_set_header  X-Real-IP         $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header  X-Forwarded-For   $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header  X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
        proxy_read_timeout                  900;
    }
}

docs.title: Git prompt cheatsheet
docs.text: Symbol Meaning * You have modified (dirty) files (unstaged changes). + You have staged changes (i.e., added to the index but not committed). % You have untracked files in your working directory. # You are on a detached HEAD (not currently on a branch). = Your working directory is clean (no changes). > Your local branch is ahead of the remote (commits to push). < Your local branch is behind the remote (commits to pull). <> Your local branch has diverged from the remote (both ahead and behind).

docs.title: Que es HTTP3
docs.text: HTTP3 se convirtió en un estandard en 2022 por IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force). La peculiaridad de este protocolo, es que corre sobre UDP gracias a QUIC (un nuevo protocolo de transporte desarrollado por Google en 2012, y luego adoptado por IETF en 2016).

docs.title: HTTP1.1
docs.text: HTTP1.1, aunque implemente pipelining, sigue recibiendo las peticiones en orden, y por eso tiene el problema de HOL a nivel de aplicación. Gracias al multiplexing de HTTP2 este problema se solucionó. ![[transport_examples_http.png]]

docs.title: HTTP2 over TCP
docs.text: Aunque HTTP2 implemente multiplexing, sigue dependiendo de TCP, y por eso aunque a nivel de aplicación se libre de HOL, a nivel de transporte sigue sufriendolo. ![[tcp_HOL.png]]

docs.title: Checking if board detects PN532
docs.text:
#include <Wire.h>

void setup() {
  Wire.begin(D2, D1);
  Serial.begin(115200);
  Serial.println("\nI2C Scanner");
}

void loop() {
  byte error, address;
  int nDevices = 0;

  for(address = 1; address < 127; address++ ) {
    Wire.beginTransmission(address);
    error = Wire.endTransmission();

    if (error == 0) {
      Serial.print("I2C device found at address 0x");
      if (address<16) Serial.print("0");
      Serial.println(address, HEX);
      nDevices++;
    }
  }
  if (nDevices == 0)
    Serial.println("No I2C devices found\n");
  else
    Serial.println("done\n");

  delay(5000);
}

docs.title: Create USB key for linux user
docs.text:
listar dispositivos 

morde@laptop ~/Desktop lsusb  
Bus 005 Device 001: ID 1d6b:0003 Linux Foundation 3.0 root hub
...

Seleccionar USB a utilizar

sudo pamusb-conf --add-device DEVICE_ID

Añadir usuario al dispositivo

sudo pamusb-conf --add-user USERNAME

Issues Doesnt fucking authenticate background services and asks for your password, for example KDE-Wallet

docs.title: List of cool tools
docs.text:
fzf -> Navigate through history dinamically inside terminal. To automatically execute fzfwhen pressing Ctrl+R in a terminal. Just add this to .bashrc/.zshrc:

source /usr/share/fzf/key-bindings.zsh

docs.title: Bash tabulation search case insensitive
docs.text:
Adding the following line to this file /etc/inputrc:

set completion-ignore-case On

docs.title: Generate .key and .cert files
docs.text: openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 365 -newkey rsa:2048 -keyout key.pem -out cert.pem

docs.title: Generate .pfx
docs.text:
Generate certificate

openssl req -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes -keyout private.key -x509 -days 365 -out certificate.crt -subj "/CN=localhost"

Encapsulate certs and create .pfx

  345  openssl pkcs12 -export   -out certificate.pfx   -inkey private.key   -in certificate.crt   -password pass:8SI1eMbT97arxo

docs.title: SSH
docs.text:
Como el ssh server solo usa RSA (creo), hay que activar temporalmente el acceso a servidores ssh con rsa (aunque éste sea vulnerable). Es para administrar el OpenWrt de forma temporal, así, no dejamos puertas abiertas a las que podrían acceder desde nuestro portátil:

ssh -oHostKeyAlgorithms=+ssh-rsa -oPubkeyAcceptedAlgorithms=+ssh-rsa -p [PORT] root@192.168.1.1

docs.title: How to Sign .EFI file Secure Boot
docs.text: This guide shows how to sign an .EFI file in order to enable secure boot and be able to boot your system (Minegrub in my case)

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: bash yay -S sbctl

docs.title: Enter Setup Mode
docs.text: This section basically means to disable secure boot and remove vendor default keys.

docs.title: Enroll key
docs.text:
Create

sbctl create-keys

And enroll

sudo sbctl enroll-keys -m

docs.title: Sign .EFI file
docs.text:
sudo sbctl sign -s /boot/vmlinuz-linux

Choose the .efi you use to boot the system.

sudo sbctl sign -s /boot/EFI/BOOT/grubx64.efi

docs.title: Automatic boot
docs.text:
Generate a new entry

sudo efibootmgr -c \
  -d /dev/sda \
  -p 1 \
  -L "Minegrub" \
  -l '\EFI\BOOT\grubx64.efi'

And change its order

sudo efibootmgr -o ID

docs.title: Enable secure boot
docs.text: Just reboot, enable secure boot and you good to go.

docs.title: Modify EFI Boot Manager
docs.text:
List entries:

sudo efibootmgr

Change boot order:

sudo efibootmgr -o 0003,0001,0000

Create new entry:

sudo efibootmgr --create --disk /dev/sda --part 1 --loader /EFI/GRUB/grubx64.efi --label "GRUB" --verbose\n
